steps.label: cause
steps.text: Tannin (polyphenolic compounds) is extracted from grape skins, seeds, or stems during maceration, or from oak during barrel ageing

steps.label: mechanism
steps.text: Tannin molecules bind to salivary proteins in the mouth and cause them to precipitate; this removes the lubricating proteins from saliva

steps.label: effect
steps.text: The palate experiences a drying, gripping, puckering tactile sensation called astringency — distinct from bitterness (a taste), which is assessed separately

steps.label: exam_formulation
steps.text: Tannins bind to salivary proteins, causing astringency — a drying, gripping sensation assessed separately from bitterness on the WSET SAT.

sat_relevance: On the SAT, tannin is assessed as a structural element (low/medium/high) and qualified (soft, grippy, chalky, silky). Astringency is not quality — ripe, integrated tannin supports quality; harsh tannin detracts from it.
